text: Minority populations are disproportionately underrepresented in most clinical studies. As a result, there is a paucity of information regarding chronic diseases that are of particular relevance to minority and other high-risk populations and precision medicine is not applicable. We identified potential reasons for underrepresentation including past mistrust, socioeconomic constraints, and language barriers. Based on our vast international experience with infectious disease studies in the last 30 years, we developed a community-based approach demonstrated with evidence obtained in the Texas Rio Grande Valley (TRGV) in the southern tip of Texas. This valley borders Mexico and is predominantly Hispanic. Our team has been conducting a longitudinal cohort study of Mexican Americans since 2004 by utilizing a community-based approach that we developed over the past 30 years conducting international studies in developing countries. The approach is based on the use of community recruited and trained members of the target population to enroll and be part of the research process. In this case, the population to be studied is the Hispanic population of the TRGV. The approach utilizes bilingual members of the same culture as the population under study. These members are recruited from the community and are trained to be outreach personnel and participants in the proposed research study. The goal of this approach is to enhance trust, simplify and decrease burden to the research participants, and to return results of the research to the community to allow participants to obtain benefits from knowledge derived from their participation. Our approach has been highly successful in obtaining participant recruitment for large multi-omic and clinical studies. The results indicate that increasing minority participation in research conducted in the US will require much more than just "tinkering" with current practices and that this challenge will only be met if investigators invest in the development of community-based research resources to foster a successful and mutually beneficial partnership between investigators and the communities they wish to study in order to enhance health equity and research advances for the benefit of all.
